problem: You have just seized a fractious city-state rife with old noble vendettas and street-level unrest. You must decide whether to purge all suspected agitators in a single, terrifying night to shock the populace into obedience, or to eliminate them slowly over many months, hoping sustained, lower-level fear avoids mass revulsion.
facets: A single purge risks creating martyrs but leaves no future enemies; a prolonged campaign keeps fear fresh yet allows potential rebels time to hide or rally.
Allies and tinpot rivals watch closely—your method signals to them whether your rule is explosive and short or calculating and enduring.
Economic life halts under mass terror, but constant disappearances corrode public trust and make daily governance unpredictable.

angles.directive: Liquidate all suspect nobles and rabble-rousers in one coordinated, overnight operation, then broadcast the finality: no further purges will come if the city remains quiet.
Bind the most dangerous malcontents to you with arranged marriages, public offices, and trade monopolies, turning former foes into invested stakeholders while ignoring minor protesters.
Hire a foreign mercenary captain to suppress disturbances independently, giving him ambiguous written orders that push all visible cruelty onto him while you appear as the merciful, distant ruler.

Stage a false conspiracy uncovered at the last hour, revealing 'traitors' among the troublemakers; their quick, 'just' execution under an emergency tribunal frames their deaths as legal defence of the state, not tyranny.

threads: I will dispatch pre-briefed kill teams with sealed dossiers to every listed residence at the same hour, liquidate all targets before sunrise, and then announce a permanent amnesty for anyone not on the list. The city absorbs one night of blood and then exchanges the corrosive uncertainty of slow disappearances for a predictable peace where obedience is the sole price of survival.

Marry the rebellious Duke Valerius’s eldest daughter to your nephew, install Valerius as Prefect of the Silk Guild with an exclusive imperial import charter, and leave the dockyard firebrands unmolested. This implants a dagger in your succession but immediately redirects the old nobility’s vendetta energy into tariff collection, halving street violence within a month.

I hire Captain Voss and his “Sunset Company” under a sealed writ authorizing “public-order measures” without specifying limits, then retire to the summer palace so every corpse found at dawn bears his sigil, not mine. This purchases me the fiction of clean hands among the nobility while the treasury hemorrhages bullion to a man who now holds a signed blank check and a garrison inside my walls.

We have Directorate Intelligence plant forged correspondence linking the top twelve agitators to a rival state’s coup plot, then convene a midnight emergency tribunal to convict and hang them within hours; this permanently poisons the judiciary’s credibility as an independent body, but the city sees the purge as righteous defense, surrendering without martyrs or prolonged bloodshed.

problem: You have annexed a proud city-state whose citizens once governed themselves. To prevent rebellion you must secure their obedience, but you cannot afford a permanent occupation garrison without bankrupting your treasury.
facets: The citizens remember self-rule and will measure every decree against their lost liberty.
Any harsh suppression will unite the leading families against you, yet leniency may be read as weakness inviting conspiracy.
Your other subject territories are watching how you handle this one—your reputation will harden or soften across your entire domain.

angles.directive: Immediately identify and publicly execute the most vocal republican sympathizers, then burn the city charter in the main square to demonstrate that their old order is dead and resistance carries a fatal price.

Select several prominent but pragmatic citizens and grant them privileged positions in your administration, binding their fortunes to your reign so they become your informants and advocates among the populace.

Appoint a loyal but ruthless outsider—a governor from a rival city—to administer the territory, letting him bear the hatred for harsh measures while you remain the distant, potentially merciful sovereign who can one day dismiss the tyrant.

Stage a grand civic festival celebrating your protection as the restoration of order and prosperity after their own factional chaos, recasting obedience not as submission but as their willing embrace of a golden peace that self-rule failed to deliver.

threads: I will have the praetorian prefect arrest the five senators who sponsored the last independence motion and behead them during the evening forum, then personally torch the bronze charter tablet before the crowd’s eyes, trading a generation of sullen hatred across this one city for an empire-wide understanding that secessionist talk ends in ash and blood.

I will appoint three former senators to the grain-tithe commission, tying their wealth to collection quotas and giving them authority to levy fines, making them my informants and the public face of taxation. They will crush dissent by identifying conspirators early, at the price of an entrenched patronage network that siphons a predictable percentage of revenue.

I install the exiled former general of their ancestral enemy as governor, granting him a private commission to extract tribute through asset seizures while I publicly condemn each excess in letters I ensure are widely circulated. This converts local hatred into fuel for a patronage cycle where I gain the city’s desperate appeals while paying only the governor’s eventual execution.

We fund a week-long Triumph of Concord by diverting the former city mint’s silver reserves into public games, grain distributions, and a new civic temple whose priesthood is drawn exclusively from the old ruling families now competing for ceremonial precedence. The treasury shrinks by a third, but the festival calendar becomes the city’s new constitution—every rite a renewal of submission disguised as ancestral pride.

problem: After recapturing rebellious Perugia, the papal captain must decide whether to execute every captured insurgent in a single morning of terror or to vanish them quietly, killing a few each month to avoid a unifying outrage.
facets: A single massacre could paralyze resistance instantly but may brand him an irredeemable tyrant and provoke foreign intervention.
Dragged-out disappearances prevent a single public martyrdom but keep the city in chronic fear, discouraging trade and stability.
Local institutions—the merchant guild, the bishop, the university—will calibrate their collaboration differently depending on whether the cruelty is a sudden thunderclap or a prolonged frost.

angles.directive: Strike all at once: line the city walls with the heads of every rebel ringleader by dawn to extinguish hope of further resistance.
Defer the bloodshed: execute only two now, keep the rest as secret hostages, and promise more deaths only if new unrest surfaces, testing time's ability to erode their cause.
Hire a harsh lieutenant to carry out the necessary disappearances over many months, then publicly execute him for his 'excesses,' channeling popular hatred onto a scapegoat.
Pardon the captured rebels, grant them lucrative city offices, and arrange marriages with your own allies to turn conspirators into partners in governance.

threads: Line the Porta Sole with the freshly severed heads of the graft-keepers and guild consuls before Matins, and then issue a single decree dissolving the university charter, thereby trading the bishop’s private condemnation for a city so psychologically shattered that every household will police itself for a generation.

He reduces the immediate blood-price to two public executions while converting the remaining insurgents into a distributed insurance policy of living hostages, guaranteeing elite collaboration through the perpetual threat of a knife reappearing in the dark. This trades the cathartic clarity of a single slaughter for a long, quiet leverage that decays the rebels' myth into forgettable grief.

Appoint a condottiero nursing a documented grudge against Perugia’s merchant guild to abduct and quietly execute two guildsmen each month, then, after a year, stage his public trial for “treasonous excesses” and behead him in the central piazza. The city’s fury buries the scapegoat, not the papacy, while the decimated guilds, grateful for the theatrical justice, resume trade under a chastened and collaborative silence.

I pardon the captured ringleaders, grant them the tax farms of Porta Sole and the salt pans, and wed their daughters to my captains, making their prosperity my garrison. The converted rebels feed me whispers preempting future plots, while my veteran soldiers grumble that their blood bought offices for the enemy.

problem: A prince has conquered the ancient free republic of Genoa, whose citizens remember their once-glorious independence and seethe under foreign rule. He must act before their secret assemblies ignite a revolt that will invite rival powers to intervene.
facets: The civic religion of liberty: many citizens would rather die free than live in servitude, making passive acceptance impossible.
The republic's deep commercial networks allow dissidents to fund insurrection covertly, using trade contacts abroad.
Neighboring rival states are eager to exploit any uprising to weaken the prince, offering ready support to rebels.

angles.directive: Demolish the city walls, execute the leading council members, and forcibly resettle the population to inland towns, ending Genoa's identity and capacity for self-rule forever.
Leave the republican forms intact but install a hand-picked Senate that rubber-stamps your decrees, avoiding direct confrontation to buy time while you consolidate power elsewhere.
Marry into the leading patrician families and grant them lucrative monopolies in your realm, turning their ambitions from liberty to profit under your protection.
Sponsor a new civic cult that glorifies the prince as the restorer of order and prosperity, gradually replacing the old foundation myths with a narrative of rebirth through submission.

threads: He will order the walls mined with gunpowder, the Council of Ten beheaded in the Piazza San Giorgio, and the entire population convoyed under cavalry escort to fortified inland towns where they will be redistributed as tenant farmers among loyalist estates. This extinguishes the republic’s identity and its covert trade-financed insurrection networks forever, at the cost of turning Europe’s most lucrative port into a sandbank haunted by silent quays.

He installs a puppet Senate of indebted merchants who pass his edicts as republican legislation. The move buys him a decade of quiet consolidation in his other territories while bleeding the republic’s commercial networks into his treasury, but it leaves the legal shell of the republic intact for any foreign-backed pretender to occupy.

He weds a Doria daughter and grants the Spinola the salt monopoly across his entire realm, while the Grimaldi receive the eastern silk franchise. The immediate loss of customs revenue buys a permanent alliance that turns the patricians’ smuggling networks into a loyalist surveillance apparatus, starving dissent of funds and foreign contact.

The prince commissions a revised foundation epic—casting himself as the divine stiller of Genoa’s storm-tossed sea-lanes—and installs a merchant-guild priesthood to recite it at all port-altars, redirecting the diaspora’s clandestine subscription networks into tax-exempt temple treasuries. This replaces the revolutionary catechism of liberty with a prosperity gospel so thoroughly that dissidents find their funding streams dried into ritual offerings, though the resulting loyalty becomes a purchased commodity susceptible to any rival who can outspend the narrative.

problem: The young heir of a conquered duchy, beloved by the populace for his father's charitable reign, lives openly in a neighboring court. Any move against him could spark a popular uprising, yet his mere existence invites conspiracies and foreign-backed restoration.
facets: The exiled heir embodies the legitimacy and cherished memory of the old ruling bloodline.
The populace's deep emotional and customary loyalty to the former dynasty makes them ripe for mobilization.
The neighboring court provides a safe haven and potential military backing, turning a domestic succession into an international lever.

angles.directive: Dispatch trusted agents to secretly poison the heir during a feast, ensuring the death appears natural and leaves no direct trace back to your hand.
Publicly dismiss the heir as an irrelevant child while privately spreading rumors that he is a foreign puppet, stalling until his novelty fades and his exile breeds contempt.
Propose a betrothal between your own son and the heir's sister, weaving the two bloodlines together to give your rule the patina of the old legitimacy.
Abolish the duchy’s ancient name and arms, divide its territory among new administrative districts, and commission chronicles that paint the deposed father as a secret tyrant.

threads: I would insert a cell of court physicians and tasters into his household under the guise of exiles seeking patronage, having them administer a slow, cumulative neurotoxin like arsenic trioxide micro-dosed in spiced wine over months to mimic a wasting fever. This burns a long-term deep-cover network that cannot be reused and demands a year of patience, yet it severs the bloodline’s future while leaving the neighboring court suspecting only their own poor hygiene or internal rivals.

The crown publicly derides the heir as a weepy boy playing at kings, while paying tavern raconteurs to paint him as a purse-stringed puppet of the host court.  
This buys three years of legitimacy-sapping inertia, at the price of a perpetual, low-burning international humiliation that must be borne silently.

We seal the succession by betrothing your son to the exiled heir’s sister, absorbing her bloodline’s legitimacy into your dynasty while relegating the heir himself to a cadet branch without a direct claim. This permanently neutralizes the restoration threat by making any foreign-backed uprising a war against the sister’s own children, at the price of granting the neighboring court a permanent familial hostage inside your palace.

The Crown issues the Edict of Dissolution, erasing the duchy’s name from royal registries, carving its lands into three new prévôtés, and funding court historians to publish the *Secret Annals of the Depraved Duke*—a documented dossier of fabricated cruelties. This trades the immediate risk of bloodshed for a generational fight over memory, securing territorial control at the price of making the chroniclers’ archives the permanent battlefield for the realm’s legitimacy.

problem: You have recently annexed a coastal province whose people speak a different tongue and worship foreign gods. The local nobles are willing to collaborate for trade privileges, but the commoners view your garrison as heretical occupiers and whisper of rebellion.
facets: The foreign tongue means your spies are easily spotted and rumors go untranslated.
Collaborating nobles can extract taxes but risk being seen as puppets, inflaming the populace further.
Your army is small, and sending reinforcements from your home territory would weaken your core defenses against rival states.

angles.directive: Marry the daughter of the most influential local noble and publicly adopt their tongue at court, fusing the ruling houses to make submission look like union.
Identify the ringleaders of whispered dissent through the nobles, then execute them all in one night, quartering your most loyal soldiers in the homes of the terrified survivors.
Grant the province self-rule under a native governor who pays you annual tribute, letting a local face absorb popular hatred while your hands stay clean and your treasury full.
Reveal an ancient prophecy or omen 'discovered' in your homeland that foretold the union of your two peoples, framing the annexation as a sacred destiny rather than a conquest.

threads: We marry Lord Varyn’s daughter and switch the court to the local tongue, merging bloodlines; ceding a quota of bureaucratic posts and our linguistic exclusivity converts their tax extraction into a joint enterprise, starving the rebellion of untranslated rumors.

The collaborating nobles will identify dissent ringleaders in exchange for trade monopolies; then, in one synchronized night, my garrison executes those named and billets soldiers in the emptied homes, turning survivors into hostage-wardens. This extinguishes overt resistance instantly but permanently embeds the army as an occupying parasite, draining local goodwill to fuel a brittle order.

We install the former chieftain’s son as tributary governor and require him to fund his own constabulary from salt tariffs, which converts popular rage from religious grievance into class resentment against a native tax collector while our garrison withdraws to a fortified offshore islet. The gain is a clean revenue stream and deniable buffer, paid for by ceding all internal policing to a regime whose survival depends entirely on our naval protection.

We dispatch court hierophants to fabricate with collaborating nobles a “lost” oracle that names our annexation as the fulfillment of their own prophecy, merging both pantheons under a single divine mandate. Staging public omens through bribed local priests drains the royal treasury but pacifies the populace without reinforcing the garrison, transforming heretical occupiers into sacred stewards.

problem: You are the new prince of a city-state, having overthrown the old ruling dynasty. The deposed lord’s 12-year-old son and his mother are in your dungeon, while the populace quietly longs for their return and neighbouring states watch for any pretext to intervene.
facets: The populace venerates the boy as the last living symbol of the old order; any open harm to him could trigger a violent uprising.
Neighbouring rulers view the child as a perfect puppet claimant and may finance a restoration attempt or use him as a casus belli.
Sparing the heir creates a permanent nucleus for conspiracy, yet executing a child and a woman risks alienating your own captains and the clergy who see it as an unforgivable atrocity.

angles.directive: Arrange a fatal 'accident' for the boy and his mother behind closed doors, then stage a public funeral at which you appear as the grieving protector, blaming a sudden fever.

Transfer the family to a remote, impregnable fortress-monastery under a foreign abbot’s custody, effectively removing them from public sight while you consolidate power and let time erode their legend.
Betroth the boy to your infant daughter and declare him your adopted heir, merging the bloodlines so that those loyal to the old house now have a stake in your own dynasty’s survival.

Tonsure the boy as a monk and promulgate a narrative that the old dynasty was decadent and illegitimate, presenting his 'voluntary' renunciation of worldly claims as a holy act that saves the city from divine wrath.

threads: I order the royal physician to administer a fast-acting poison masked as a sleeping draught to both prisoners tonight, then publicly display their fever-wracked bodies tomorrow while I weep beside the catafalque. This purchases immediate dynastic security and foreign non-intervention at the permanent price of a whisper that never dies among the servants who prepared the shrouds.

I transfer the widow and heir to the cliffside monastery of San Verian under the personal oath-bond of its neutral Tuscan abbot, removing them from domestic reach while branding any rescue as foreign sacrilege. This buys a decade of fading memory at the price of permanently ceding physical custody to an actor I cannot command.

I announce a formal betrothal between the deposed lord’s 12-year-old son and my newborn daughter, simultaneously adopting him into my house as crown prince under a regency that dissolves his independent claim. This fuses the old bloodline’s symbolic legitimacy into my dynasty’s succession, turning every conspirator who would use him into a defender of my infant heir’s future throne while the cost is a permanent hostage-prince at my own table whom I must now educate into a loyal rival rather than a corpse.

I compel the boy at knifepoint to recite monastic vows before a handpicked assembly, then flood the city with canon-law tracts declaring his "holy forfeiture" binds heavenly mercy to my reign; the immediate gain is that any rebel now attacks a living saint, but I have made the prior the sole keeper of a secret that can unmake me.

problem: A prince has inherited a vulnerable city-state surrounded by expansionist rivals; his treasury cannot sustain mercenaries indefinitely, his subjects resist conscription, and his powerful neighbor offers auxiliaries in exchange for a permanent alliance that would reduce him to a client.
facets: The loyalty of mercenaries evaporates when pay is late, leaving the state defenseless at the worst moment.
A citizen militia requires arming the populace, which could ignite a revolt if they feel oppressed or see the prince as weak.
Auxiliaries bring the ambitions of a foreign power inside the walls, risking annexation after the immediate threat passes.

angles.directive: Eliminate all reliance on external forces by forcibly conscripting every able-bodied man through terror, purging potential dissidents, and creating a brutal citizen army forged through harsh discipline and constant campaign.

Defer the final choice by hiring both mercenaries and auxiliaries on short-term contracts, playing them against each other while secretly fueling rivalries among their captains to prevent any one force from dominating, buying time to build a war chest.

Co-opt the local nobility and urban guilds by granting them military commands in a mixed force: a core of citizen-militia from loyal districts, supplemented by a small, carefully selected mercenary contingent under the prince's direct pay, all funded by new taxes on the co-opted elites.

Reframe the state’s defense as a sacred civic cult, transforming the militia into a vehicle of virtue and propaganda that redefines citizenship around martial service, attracts volunteers through honor and religious fervor, and isolates dissenters as impious traitors.

threads: He orders mass roundups of draft-age men at spearpoint, publicly executes resisters and suspected agitators in the central square, then breaks the survivors into legions through decimation and ceaseless punitive drills, forging a garrison-state army whose internal terror eclipses any foreign threat while permanently extinguishing his population’s will to revolt.

He hires both mercenary companies and the neighbour’s auxiliaries on rolling thirty-day contracts, then feeds each captain forged letters implicating the other in a planned mutiny, while channeling the resulting standoff into a bidding war for his favour. The treasury bleeds at double rate yet the city buys exactly the one-year window needed to convert emergency grain monopolies into a war chest independent of either faction.

The prince imposes a dedicated military-patronage levy on guild masters and noble estates, granting them captaincies over district-militia levies while retaining a small, directly stipended mercenary column under his sole purse. This fuses elite wealth to regime survival and fragments armed manpower across rival commanders, yet permanently cedes fiscal initiative to the very oligarchs it elevates.

The prince declares defense a sacred rite, reserving citizenship, land grants, and weapon-rights to those initiated into a state cult through a public military ordeal, while branding resisters as impious traitors whose confiscated estates finance the militia. This replaces mercenary payrolls with fanatical volunteers but permanently transfers the locus of legitimacy from the throne to the warrior-priesthood, risking a theocratic coup.

problem: You have conquered the Duchy of Sermoneta, whose former duke escaped to the Holy Roman Emperor, leaving his 8-year-old son hidden in a local Benedictine abbey. The populace still murmurs the old duke's name and pays taxes only under armed escort, while the boy remains a living symbol ripe for imperial exploitation. How do you neutralize the boy as a rallying point without inflaming the people or the Church?
facets: The boy's bloodline is a living symbol of the old order, easily activated by the Emperor to justify invasion or insurrection.
The people's loyalty is sentimental, rooted in memories of the former duke's charity and justice, not in your new regime's strength or benefits.
The abbey is sacred ground; any violation could provoke ecclesiastical censure and turn the pious populace violently against you.

angles.directive: Send a trusted agent to smother the boy in his sleep and spread a rumor that he died of a sudden fever, burying him quietly before the abbot can raise an alarm.

Publicly 'discover' the boy, declare him your honored ward, and betroth him to your infant daughter, merging the bloodlines so that any rebellion against you becomes a rebellion against the duke's own heir.
Persuade the abbot to proclaim the boy's mystical visions, then have the boy take solemn monastic vows, forever removing him from political eligibility as one consecrated to God.

Anonymously pay a band of foreign mercenaries to abduct the boy and deliver him to the Emperor's court, then loudly denounce the Emperor for kidnapping an innocent child, uniting the people in outrage against the external foe.

threads: I dispatch my personal physician, whose medical authority legitimizes a swift death certificate, to administer a concentrated dose of monkshood during the boy’s sleep, then have him buried in an unmarked grave within the abbey crypt before dawn. The Church’s own ground swallows the symbol, buying silence through complicity while trading a short-lived murmur of suspicious grief for the permanent erasure of an imperial casus belli.

I will enroll the boy under a fabricated papal indulgence as "Duke-Consort Designate," binding his hereditary claim to my daughter in a ceremony conducted inside the abbey itself, forcing the monks to sacralize the merger. This drains the Emperor's capacity to wield the boy as a rival banner by making any imperial restoration an attack on the boy's own unborn dynasty, at the cost of permanently embedding his bloodline's claim in my succession.

Offer the abbot the deed to the contested mill and a bishopric for his nephew in return for fabricating the boy's Marian visions and immediately tonsuring him into solemn Benedictine vows. This transforms the boy into a politically inert holy fool, forever barred from inheritance, at the cost of a mill and a bishop's mitre.

I hire the Black Company’s freelance outriders through a cut-out Genoese factor to snatch the boy from the abbey gardens at night, then dispatch ambassadors to every Italian court decrying the Emperor’s brazen kidnapping, which costs me the mercenary fee and the risk of discovery but converts the boy’s symbolic power into a galvanizing casus belli against imperial aggression, not my rule.

problem: A prince conquers a culturally distinct border province that has repeatedly rebelled. He must choose between constructing an overwhelming fortress to dominate the region or dismantling all fortifications and cultivating the people's goodwill through leniency and public works.
facets: The fortress drains the treasury, diverting funds from public improvements that could win hearts.
A stronghold signals mistrust to the populace, potentially deepening resentment and inviting conspiracy.
Neighboring powers may view a fortress as a provocation, drawing external threats that goodwill could deter.

angles.directive: Raze the most disloyal district, erect the fortress on its ruins, and execute vocal dissenters to crush rebellion instantly.
Appoint a governor to assess loyalties for a year, withdraw to your capital, and defer the fortress decision while avoiding immediate responsibility.
Grant the province's merchant guilds full citizenship and council seats, binding them with privileges instead of walls.
Fund a 'People's Bastion' against a common foreign menace, staffed by local militia and presented as their own defense rather than a symbol of oppression.

threads: The prince orders the Old Quarter razed and a citadel erected on its foundations, while his guard publicly hangs the twelve known ringleaders. This liquidates a generation of dissent at the price of one district’s destruction, transforming the province’s memory into a deterrent that outlasts any garrison.

He installs Governor Terentius with a census team to map landholdings, tax grievances, and clandestine kinship ties for twelve months while the prince’s court feigns disinterest, freezing both fortress construction and demilitarization. This pause converts the treasury into a liquidity reserve that buys intelligence, yet cedes the narrative battlefield to local elites who will harden patronage networks in the vacuum.

The prince grants the five dominant merchant guilds hereditary council seats, tariff veto, and full citizenship for oath-sworn members. The price is a mercantile bloc with permanent fiscal veto, but that dependency converts their trade networks into an unpaid intelligence apparatus and their treasuries into a self-replenishing defense fund against external threats.

The prince diverts the fortress budget to arm and train a “Border Watch” militia against the steppe khanate that raids both the province and its neighbors, appointing local elders as officers and funding the corps through a new provincial defense tithe. This converts the garrison from an occupation symbol into a shared bulwark, binding the region through external threat while the tithe’s burden is offset by the prince’s withdrawal of imperial tax collectors for a decade.

problem: A new prince has seized a wealthy coastal duchy with French backing, but his French auxiliaries now garrison the citadel and demand permanent concessions, while local nobles offer to fund a citizen militia if he expels the foreigners. He must choose the foundation of his military power.
facets: Auxiliaries (French) are professionally effective but their loyalty belongs to an external monarch who may turn them against the prince at will.
Citizen-arms preserve independence and build domestic legitimacy, but are untested, slow to train, and risk arming the very populace that may resent his rule.
Mercenaries offer an immediate alternative to both, but their captains may seek their own power or desert for higher pay, leaving the state defenseless mid-conflict.

angles.directive: Secretly poison the French captain and key officers during a victory feast, then seize the citadel gates with your personal guard, disarming the leaderless auxiliaries and expelling them before their king can react.

Grant the French auxiliaries land and tax exemptions to settle as a permanent foreign colony, intermarrying them with local noble families so their interests fuse with the duchy's, transforming them into a hereditary loyalist caste.

Invite a rival Italian power (Milan or Venice) to 'mediate' the French withdrawal by offering them a trade monopoly in return for expelling the auxiliaries, letting another state absorb the French king's retaliation while you stand as the wronged host appealing to Italian solidarity.

Announce a grand civic tournament and public oath-swearing, declaring the militia a sacred 'Order of the Duchy' rather than a conscript army—arming citizens not as soldiers but as sworn knights of a new chivalric brotherhood, binding their honor to your person while the French auxiliaries are dismissed as unworthy of the holy charge.

threads: Feast-night, the prince’s cupbearer doses the French captain and senior lieutenants with a slow emetic masked in spiced wine, then his household knights seal the hall and cut down the retching officers before the citadel garrison learns of the slaughter; the leaderless auxiliaries are rounded up at dawn and marched to the harbor, trading a single night of butchery and a permanent stain on his honor for an emptied fortress and a throne no foreign king can unmake.

The prince issues a charter converting the French garrison into a hereditary military colony, granting each captain a landed estate with perpetual tax exemption contingent on immediate marriage to a local noble daughter. This surgically severs their dependence on the French crown, exchanging permanent fiscal loss and a few offended native houses for a self-replicating loyalist caste whose private wealth is now the realm’s frontier defense.

Deliver the French garrison to a Milanese expeditionary force disguised as “neutral observers” under a pre-signed condotta, then publicly protest their “unsanctioned” eviction while secretly granting Milan duty-free warehousing at the port. Milan absorbs Paris’s punitive embargo as the cost of a permanent commercial foothold, while the prince inherits a cleansed citadel and the political cover to arm his own militia behind a screen of Italian solidarity.

He proclaims a grand tourney to knight the citizenry as the ‘Order of the Duchy,’ publicly denounces the French as unworthy, and marches them to the border. The immediate cost is the loss of trained garrisons; the gain is a chivalric bond that turns every shopkeeper into a personal hostage against rebellion.

problem: You are the royal preceptor, revered by your students—the crown prince and his exiled cousins alike; the king now commands you to lead his legions against those very students, who have raised an army to claim their birthright.
facets: The guru–disciple bond is sacred, akin to parenthood, yet the king’s authority is absolute and your oath of service binds you to his cause.
Your students on the opposing side fight for dharma, exposing the adharma of the throne you serve.
Refusing the command may brand you a traitor and endanger your own lineage, while obeying destroys the very souls you have nurtured.

angles.directive: Openly defy the king’s command, join your students’ righteous rebellion, and seek to eliminate the corrupt court through direct martial support.
Withdraw into the forest, announcing that a guru cannot witness the slaughter of his own shishyas; let both sides fight without your presiding shadow.
Use your leverage to force a dynastic integration—broker a marriage alliance or a partition treaty that merges both claims before battle is joined.

Accept the command but transform the war into a dharmic trial: declare that you will only fight defensively and without fatal intent, turning the battlefield into a final teaching of restraint and consequence.

threads: I will march the royal household guard to the rebel camp at dawn, publicly kneeling before the crown prince as my new sovereign and handing him the citadel’s gate-passcodes and patrol rotations, which collapses the capital’s outer defenses within a single night. This severs my family from royal patronage forever but transforms a ragged insurgency into an immediate, throne-room-ready fighting force.

I pre-empt the king’s order by publicly renouncing my station and retreating to the Dandaka forest at dawn, declaring that a preceptor who midwifed his pupils’ souls cannot preside over their dismemberment. This move surrenders my political lineage to charges of cowardice but preserves the guru–shishya sanctity as an unbroken cultural precedent that delegitimizes both the throne’s coercion and the rebels’ demand for my blessing.

I force the king’s hand by threatening to reveal his bastardy unless he betroths the crown prince to the rebel leader’s daughter and cedes half the kingdom, thereby averting war. My blackmail stains my soul, yet the students live and the dynasty integrates.

I will deploy the *Kaurava Gambit*: announce before both armies that my blade strikes only steel, never flesh, and that every wound I inflict shall be cauterized by my own hand before sunset—thereby converting each clash into a live dissection of consequence where the prince learns that even a just war cannot be waged without bleeding its own surgeon dry. This erases my military utility entirely while preserving my seat as the one teacher who made the battlefield itself a syllabus written in his own opened veins.

problem: You have conquered the maritime republic of Venetia, a prosperous city-state whose citizens have governed themselves for centuries. They resent your rule and covertly fund exiles, but you desperately need its trade revenues and fleet to finance your campaigns; any harsh crackdown risks a revolt you cannot quickly suppress, while leniency may allow conspiracy to flourish.
facets: The republic's civic identity is built on a myth of liberty, and its citizens treat self-governance as a sacred inheritance, making them quick to resent any foreign impositions.
The city's wealth is generated by merchant networks that require stability and trust; if you disrupt commerce by purging the merchant class, you destroy the very asset you came to possess.
Your occupying army is small and overstretched, and the city's layout—a maze of canals and fortified compounds—gives local militias a natural advantage in guerrilla resistance.

angles.directive: Raze the city to the ground, scatter its population to the countryside, and resettle the site with loyal colonists, erasing the republic's memory so completely that no one will think of liberty again.

Install a figurehead council of local merchants, grant them nominal self-rule under a light tribute, and withdraw your main force to the mainland, letting them exhaust their rebellious fervor in harmless assemblies while you slowly tighten your grip from a distance.

Offer full citizenship and senatorial seats to the leading families, intermarry with them, and extend your patronage to the guilds, making the city's prosperity dependent on your favor and turning its elites into stakeholders in your rule.

Proclaim yourself the restorer of their ancient liberties, framing the conquest as liberation from a corrupt oligarchy; establish a new popular assembly you control, and rewrite the city's founding myth through public festivals to enshrine your dynasty as the true guardians of their freedom.

threads: I order the systematic demolition of Venetia’s central districts—razing the Arsenal, the Grand Canal palaces, and the mercantile exchanges—then scatter the surviving populace across inland agricultural settlements under military overseers, permanently breaking their urban commercial identity. This extinguishes the republic’s myth at the price of the city’s immediate tax yield, while converting a restive naval power into a network of docile farming hamlets that supply grain and conscripts for future campaigns.

I will appoint a Council of Ten from the merchant elite, grant them ceremonial authority over guilds and festivals, and withdraw my garrison to Mestre, levying a fixed tribute of silver and ships. The tribute finances my campaigns while the council’s inevitable factional deadlock erodes the republic’s myth of liberty, turning them into dependent clients who will eventually beg for my military intervention to restore order.

We grant the three hundred wealthiest merchant houses hereditary patrician status and reserve one-third of the new Senate seats for their nominees, while betrothing your heir to the Doge’s granddaughter to fuse dynastic legitimacy with imperial protection. This permanently ties their ledger books to your treasury but surrenders the palace guard to a joint command structure that can paralyze the city the moment your armies march east.

You install a puppet popular assembly that ritually "votes" to revoke the oligarchs' ancient property titles and redistributes their estates to the citizenry, binding the masses to your dynasty through material gratitude. This permanently liquidates the old elite’s financial capacity to fund exiles at the price of making every future merchant fortune dependent on your continued patronage.

problem: A newly appointed censor has obtained a former servant’s testimony linking the household to illegal salt smuggling and the murder of a witness, and now threatens to reopen the sealed case unless the household pays an enormous, recurring tribute.
facets: The household’s liquid reserves are dangerously low after lavish spending on the garden, feasts, and bribes for other officials, making even a single payment crippling and a recurring levy ruinous.
The censor is ambitious, secretly corrupt but publicly known for exposing malefactors; defying him risks immediate indictment, yet paying risks an endless spiral of blackmail.

The murder involved the household's favored concubine as an accomplice; any official investigation would rip apart the inner quarters, destroy her reputation, and turn family members against one another.

angles.directive: Confront the censor with seized evidence of his own past bribe-taking and threaten to deliver it to his superiors unless he withdraws, creating a mutual destruction standoff.

Stall by sending a trusted steward to deliver partial payments and false promises, while secretly shifting incriminating goods out of the compound and waiting for the censor’s term to end or his sudden removal.
Offer the censor a permanent share in the household’s smuggling network, turning him into a silent business partner whose own prosperity depends on the household's continued illegal trade.

Reframe the witness murder as a righteous act to protect the household from a treacherous servant’s extortion, and propose that the censor publicly memorialize the case as justifiable self-preservation in exchange for a generous one-time 'gift' that elevates his moral standing.

threads: We deliver a sealed dossier of his past bribe receipts to the censor’s private residence with a message: withdraw the case or these reach the capital by courier at dawn. The standoff freezes his ambition—he cannot indict us without guaranteeing his own execution—but it permanently weaponizes our secret archive, making any future reconciliation impossible.

The steward will arrive with a single jade box containing one-third of the demanded sum and a ledger of fabricated household debts, apologizing profusely while requesting a sixty-day audit delay that coincidentally spans the censor’s expected transfer window. That same night, the concubine’s bloodied silks and the salt-crusted storage jars travel by separate oxcarts to a distant cousin’s estate, converting a ruinous recurring levy into a one-time logistical burn and buying the household just enough calendar to outlast the man rather than his price.

They permanently install the censor as a silent equity partner in the smuggling operation by structuring his cut as a percentage of gross cargo value laundered through a fictive caravan investment, which converts his blackmail into self-interested protection while the household sacrifices a fixed slice of future illicit revenue to immunize the sealed case against reopening.

The steward petitions the censor to memorialize the killing as righteous defense against a treacherous servant’s extortion, funding a ‘Hall of Moral Rectitude’ in his name to cement the ruling. This liquidates the garden’s jade reserves but permanently shackles the censor’s reputation to the household’s innocence, transforming his blackmail leverage into a career-defining public virtue.

problem: You have seized a hereditary duchy whose young exiled heir, sheltered by a rival kingdom, commands the people's lingering devotion; any overt cruelty could ignite a rebellion your modest garrison cannot suppress.
facets: The old bloodline is revered as sacred and divinely ordained, making any violent act against the heir a potential trigger for mass uprising and external intervention.
Your army is small, partially mercenary, and dependent on steady pay, so prolonged repression or heavy taxation to fund it risks mutiny or defection to the rival.
The rival state protecting the heir is poised to exploit internal chaos, and local nobles secretly maintain loyalty to the old house while outwardly submitting.

angles.directive: Secretly eliminate the exiled heir by poisoned blade or arranged accident, severing the bloodline's hope and forcing the people to accept the new reality.
Fortify the key cities, withdraw from the countryside, and let time erode popular passion; avoid provocations while the heir's symbolic power fades without action.
Marry a daughter of the deposed house, adopt its heraldry and customs, and declare a union of dynasties, transforming the conquest into a restoration.
Flood the duchy with proclamations and public spectacles that expose the old dynasty's hidden crimes, framing your rule as liberation from tyranny and rewriting collective memory.

threads:
I dispatch a eunuch-poisoner, once a relic-keeper in their own temple, to anoint the heir’s prayer beads with a contact toxin that triggers fatal seizure during the Night Vigil, framing the rival king’s jealous courtiers.  
This severs the sacred bloodline’s visible hope at the cost of the agent’s guaranteed torture and execution, collapsing divine-right sentiment into a leaderless, demoralized vacuum that our garrison can pacify without firing a shot.

By withdrawing all garrisons to the three walled river ports and halting tax collection in the hinterland, you convert the occupation into an invisible, costless absentee claim that starves the heir’s legend of martyred defiance, trading territorial presence for fiscal solvency and time.

I will wed the exiled heir’s sister to my own son under a public rite that re-consecrates our merged lineage through the old house’s sacred regalia, thereby transforming my usurpation into a legally unbroken succession that defangs both divine reverence and rival casus belli. This gambit permanently dissolves the bloodline’s insurrectionary pull into my own dynasty, but at the price of ceding half the council seats and all future heirs’ naming rights to the deposed house’s customs.

I stage a traveling inquisition with actors swearing witnessed oaths to the old duke’s infanticides, distributing woodcut prints; the campaign diverts mercenary wages, risking desertion, but it poisons memory, making the exiled heir’s name evoke a shudder and the rival’s protection complicity.

problem: A Gu Immortal must choose whether to attempt refining the forbidden 'Bloodline Severance Gu'. The refinement has only a 1 in 10 chance of success and requires the guaranteed destruction of his own immortal aperture as the main ingredient, yet it is the only known way to purge the bloodline curse dooming his entire clan.
facets: The clan's extinction is certain without the cure, but the immortal's sacrifice would leave the clan leaderless even if successful.
The refinement process demands the aperture as material, meaning the immortal would permanently lose all cultivation and Gu worms, becoming a mortal.
A rival faction is secretly pushing for the attempt, hoping to manipulate the outcome and seize the clan's resources regardless.

angles.directive: Attempt the refinement, sacrificing your immortal aperture in the hope of purging the curse.
Hunt down the hidden Gu Immortal who planted the bloodline curse and eliminate them before the curse fully manifests.
Abandon the clan to its fate, faking your death and fleeing to a remote region with your aperture intact.

Publicly announce the refinement plan to attract the rival faction’s investment, then covertly refine a Gu that merely suppresses the curse for a century, using their resources and exposing them as scapegoats if it fails.

threads: I will feed the aperture directly into the Bloodsea Crucible at the moment of lunar eclipse, baiting the rival faction into committing their infiltration force to a ritual they falsely believe they control, only to catalyze a refinement chain that annihilates the curse’s vector before the aperture’s collapse triggers a clan-wide bloodline resonance cascade—shattering our spiritual foundation permanently but purging the doom from every living descendant.

Deploying the Bloodline Resonance Compass, he pinpoints the curse-planter, then sacrifices his Soulsever Thorn Gu to execute an instant kill. The strike costs a decade of lifespan and the Gu itself, but annihilates the curse's origin before manifestation, saving the clan under his continued rule.

He triggers a False Dawn Metamorphosis Gu to simulate a failed breakthrough explosion, then rides the chaos out on a smuggler’s wind-whale to the uncharted Silent Isles. The rival faction inherits a leaderless husk while he retains the aperture they coveted, turning their scheme into his escape vehicle.

He broadcasts the refinement ritual as a joint salvation effort to extract the rival faction’s vast resources, then secretly refines a Century Suppression Gu that bypasses aperture destruction entirely; the rival’s investment becomes exposed evidence of their sabotage when the curse re-emerges, costing them their covert network while buying the clan a century and preserving his own cultivation base.

problem: A rank 4 cultivator, Hei Li, has infiltrated the Blood Sea Sect and is bound by a Heart Demon Oath to obey Sect Leader Xue's direct orders. Xue orders him to guard the sacrificial formation's perimeter during the Blood God Ritual, knowing that the formation will consume all life within it—including Hei Li—and the oath will kill him if he disobeys or flees.
facets: The Heart Demon Oath senses the bearer's intent: even entertaining thoughts of violating an order can trigger a lethal backlash, forcing absolute mental discipline.
The Blood God Ritual requires a stabilizer Gu worm that Hei Li could potentially tamper with, but the sect leader carries the core activator and monitors the formation closely.
Rival elder Black Bone has long coveted the sect leader's position and might strike during the ritual, creating a window of chaos that could either kill Hei Li faster or offer a chance to slip away.

angles.directive: Arrange for Black Bone to assassinate Sect Leader Xue at the ritual's climax using forged evidence of betrayal, counting on the chaos to void the order and survive the oath's dissolution.
Exploit a loophole in the order 'guard the eastern gate' by interpreting 'guard' as patrolling the outer perimeter, stepping outside the formation's kill zone while technically complying.
Secretly plant a parasitic Life Absorption Gu inside the formation core, so that when the ritual activates, it diverts the life force to you instead, turning the sacrifice into a cultivation boon.
Fake a beast tide warning using a sound-mimic Gu, forcing the sect leader to issue a new overriding order to defend the sect, thereby suspending the guard duty and buying time to escape the oath.

threads: Hei Li plants a forged blood-seal missive detailing Xue’s intent to sacrifice Black Bone during the ritual, timing its discovery to trigger Black Bone’s assassination at the exact moment the stabilizer Gu is exposed. Xue’s death voids the oath, but the collapsing formation’s backlash severs Hei Li’s meridian permanently, trading a decisive enemy for a crippled path forward.

He walks the tree line sixty paces beyond the eastern gate’s outer markers, genuinely framing ‘guard’ as external perimeter patrol to intercept encroachers; that physical distance voids the formation’s sacrifice radius, trading the gatehouse’s protective wards for a corpse-free post‑ritual alive status.

Hei Li embeds a parasitic Life Absorption Gu into the formation’s stabilizer array during a perimeter patrol, framing the insertion as a reinforcement measure to escape the oath’s intent detection. When the ritual ignites, the Gu siphons the sacrificial life force into his dantian, granting an instant breakthrough while leaving him shackled by the oath’s kill-switch the moment Xue perceives the theft.

Hei Li activates a pre-placed sound-mimic Gu to broadcast a Silverback Thunder Ape’s territorial shriek, tricking the sect’s early-warning arrays into declaring a beast tide; Sect Leader Xue’s emergency mass defense order immediately overwrites the perimeter guard directive, suspending the Oath’s trigger and allowing Hei Li to vanish into the mobilized defenders, at the cost of his last stealth asset and the near-certain ignition of Black Bone’s coup during the scramble.

problem: You have conquered a prosperous city-state accustomed to republican self-rule. Its citizens resent your authority and murmur of restoration, but their skilled artisans and trade networks are essential to your treasury. You must decide whether to rule through harsh exemplary terror that paralyzes dissent, or through lenient paternalism that courts genuine affection.
facets: The city's compact layout means any unrest can rapidly concentrate at the central piazza, making swift suppression physically challenging.
Their guilds maintain communication networks with sister republics abroad, capable of soliciting foreign intervention if grievances fester.
Your garrison commander, a loyal brute who rose through the ranks, openly advocates a night of selective atrocities to break their spirit now rather than bleed later.

angles.directive: Stage a public execution of the most vocal republican ringleaders on charges of conspiring with a foreign power. Make attendance mandatory. Display their severed heads on the guild hall until the stench compels submission.

Marry into the most influential merchant family and appoint their patriarch as your chancellor. Fund a magnificent civic festival celebrating your union, redistributing seized republican assets as dowry gifts to the guilds.

Invite a neighboring condottiero to install a podestà drawn from a rival city to administer justice and taxation. Let the hired governor absorb the hatred for austerity measures while you remain aloof as the ultimate court of merciful appeal.

Declare the republic dissolved not as conquest but as rescue from oligarchic corruption. Invent a hereditary title for yourself as 'Protector of the Popolo,' and stage-manage acclamations in the piazza where common laborers are paid to publicly petition you to stay and rule them directly.

threads: I will have the garrison commander behead the seven most vocal republicans on falsified foreign conspiracy charges, force all guild members to witness, and spike the heads on the guild hall. This costs me a dozen skilled glassblowers who flee overnight, but gains a silenced city whose trade routes fill my coffers without the friction of dissent.

I marry the Vespucci patriarch’s daughter and install him as chancellor, then liquidate seized republican estates to fund a union festival that distributes the proceeds as dowry gifts to each guild. This purchases the guilds’ foreign networks as dynastic assets, at the cost of ceding permanent treasury oversight to a merchant council.

I contract the Orsini condottiero to impose a Perugian jurist as podestà, funding his mercenary court solely through a new hearth-tax he collects; the city’s rage fixes on his collectors and foreign accent, while I overturn his harshest verdicts from a hill villa, draining the treasury by a third but converting republican murmurs into petitions for my protection.

I pronounce the old Senate dissolved as a cabal of merchant-oligarchs and accept the hereditary mantle of Protector of the Popolo, staging a piazza spectacle where hired day-laborers publicly kneel and clamor for my direct rule. This purchased acclamation transforms them into visible collaborators whose safety now depends on my regime's survival, turning the city's poorest into a self-recruiting informant network at the price of a permanently subsidized bread-and-circus slush fund.

problem: A new prince has seized a disordered city-state riddled with feuding families. To secure it, he must inflict cruelties. Should he commit all necessary acts at once to crush dissent immediately, or spread them out gradually to avoid the stain of a single atrocity?
facets: The immediate shock of a single massacre could paralyze opposition, but might also unite survivors in desperate hatred.
Spreading cruelties over time allows the prince to calibrate punishment to unfolding threats, but risks prolonging instability and allowing conspiracies to form.
Neighboring powers watch closely: a swift, terrible act may deter external intervention, while a drawn-out approach could invite foreign meddling under the guise of protecting the people.

angles.directive: Execute all identified troublemakers and their families in a single, public night of terror, leaving no doubt about the price of rebellion.
Spread the cruelties out over months, punishing malcontents only after specific provocations, so each act seems a measured response rather than a reign of terror.
Instead of bloodshed, offer the leading factions key offices and tax farms, binding their fortunes to the new regime and redirecting their ambitions into patronage.
Stage a grand public tribunal where the most hated faction leaders are tried for ancient crimes, transforming the executions into a popular ritual of justice that purges the city’s corruption.

threads: The prince’s guard beheads the patriarchs of all seven houses and their kin in a single torchlit hour, mounting their heads on the gates by dawn. This purchases a generation of peace by transforming the memory into a self-replenishing deterrent, while consuming the indigenous governing class and forcing the prince to import foreign administrators.

He instructs his spymaster to feed each major house a forged invitation to a fictitious foreign conspiracy on a staggered monthly schedule, then arrests and executes that house’s leadership for “treason” before a tribunal of their rivals. This ritualized jurisprudence prevents unified rebellion by making every punishment appear a reluctantly accepted necessity, but gradually consumes his own intelligence apparatus as each source is sacrificed, leaving him blind to genuine external meddling.

He awards the three strongest merchant houses exclusive tax-farming rights over the port, grain, and customs revenues, while seating their elders on a newly formed Council of State that divides ministerial portfolios among them. This embeds their wealth in the regime’s survival, turning each house into a hostile watchdog against any conspiracy that might disrupt the revenue streams they now command, at the price of permanently conceding a fifth of state income to private extraction.

The prince convenes the Court of Public Expiation, exhuming sealed testimony of the faction leaders’ old murders and rapes, then lets the crowd vote on their guilt before a ritual beheading in the main square. This spectacle channels popular vengeance into allegiance, costing the prince the flexibility of secret purges but buying a city unified by shared bloodlust.

problem: You have seized a duchy whose young prince escaped into the countryside, where peasants still light candles for his family. You need stability, but every tax collector you send meets silence or sabotage.
facets: The escaped heir is a focal point for resistance, not its organizer—eliminating him might create a martyr without ending the dysfunction.
Local notables publicly swear fealty but privately fund the prince's exile and whisper that your rule is a passing storm.
The populace’s loyalty to the old bloodline is sentimental and religious, not rational, making punishment inflame rather than pacify.

angles.directive: Hunt the escaped prince across every village and sanctuary—real or rumored—and execute him publicly, then settle loyal colonists in the most resistant hamlets to break the memory of his line.

Negotiate the prince’s return under a ceremonial title with estates and a pension, then marry him into your own family to fuse the bloodlines and drain his political significance while keeping him visible and dependent.

Sponsor a new civic cult honoring an ancient local saint whose feast coincides with the founding of your rule, reframing the calendar so that loyalty to you becomes inseparable from sacred tradition and the old dynasty seems a deviation.

Petition a neighboring power—perhaps the Church or an imperial authority—to declare the old dynasty illegitimate for some ancient heresy or debt, then govern as their appointed viceroy so that resentment flows upward past you.

threads: By paying informants with confiscated tithe-grain and compelling local notables to witness the prince's beheading in the main square, we tie their survival to our permanence; afterward, we settle demobilized soldiers with tax-free land in the most candle-lit villages, converting shrines into storehouses so that subsistence, not sentiment, orders their days.

I will grant the prince the ceremonial Duchy of the Uplands with a walled manor and a lifetime pension, then marry him to my daughter.  
The fused bloodline transforms him into a living, pensioned sign of continuity whose very visibility drains the mystique of exile into a gilded cage; this costs one estate and a marriage but secures the duchy’s revenues without a single burning village.

By chartering the Order of Saint Aldric with exclusive rights to consecrate the harvest on the anniversary of our victory, we absorb the peasant’s candle-lit vigils into a state-funded liturgical calendar that paints the old bloodline as usurpers of a far older sanctity. This permanently transfers ancestral legitimacy from blood to rite at the price of funding endless feast days, gaining a populace that experiences any tax collector’s arrival as a liturgical procession.

I petition the Archprelate of the Holy See to promulgate a bull of anathema against the prince’s bloodline, retroactively voiding their coronation oaths over a seventh-century suppression of monastics, then accept investiture as hereditary viceroy so that peasant candle-lightings become acts of schism, not sedition. The cost is surrendering a third of the duchy’s tithe revenue in perpetuity to a distant throne, but it transforms local defiance into theological error that the parish clergy must police for you.

problem: Having seized the proud merchant republic of Virenta, Prince Dorian must decide whether to govern his new subjects through exemplary terror to deter dissent, or through generous leniency to foster loyalty and prevent economic flight.
facets: Virenta’s citizens have a generations-long tradition of elective governance; any sign of foreign control is seen as an affront to their honor, making them quick to interpret mercy as contemptible weakness.
The city’s wealthy trading houses control the arterial commerce of the region—if they flee or withdraw their capital, the principality’s treasury and supply lines will collapse.
Three rival city-states in the same league are scrutinizing Dorian’s methods, ready to fund a rebellion if Virenta appears either too cruelly crushed or too laxly held.

angles.directive: Publicly execute the leading republican families, install an imposing fortress garrison, and proclaim that any dissent will be met with the swift destruction of entire households and their commercial assets.

Withdraw the permanent garrison, grant Virenta nominal independence under a tributary treaty, and rely on its governing council’s fear of the neighboring league to keep it aligned, thus sidestepping the direct fear/love dilemma altogether.

Elevate the city’s preeminent merchants to counts of the realm, intermarry with their leading daughters, and give them privileged monopolies on key trade routes so that their fortunes become inseparable from your dynasty’s survival.

Present yourself not as a conqueror but as the liberator who has overthrown a corrupt oligarchy, establish a new popular assembly with symbolic honors, and sponsor grand festivals that recast your rule as the restoration of ancient Virentan glory.

threads: I will have the Iron Chamberlain publicly behead the heads of the five principal trading houses in the Speaker’s Square at dawn, then seal the harbor granaries under a garrison of 800 household troops while issuing a decree that any house caught moving bullion or correspondence to the league rivals forfeits its entire bloodline and warehouse inventory to the crown. This move trades the immediate flight of a third of the merchant fleet for the permanent paralysis of the league’s ability to fund an organized insurgency, because the surviving houses will calculate that rebellion requires a capital base the state now physically controls.

Prince Dorian dissolves his garrison and signs a tributary treaty recognizing the Virentan council’s sovereignty. He secretly feeds intelligence to the rival league about the city’s newfound weakness, goading them into threatening maneuvers that force the council to cling to his protection for survival—trading direct military expense for a self-enforcing hostage dynamic.

He confers hereditary county titles on the three dominant trading houses, seals each pact with a state marriage to their eldest daughters, and awards their fleets exclusive royal charters for the eastern spice lanes, thereby dissolving republican identity into dynastic ambition while the cost of their ennoblement—permanent aristocratic privilege—buys a cartel whose collapse now means your collapse.

Dorian erects a Popular Assembly from the artisan guilds, confers archaic titles, and spends the city’s reserve on the Liberator’s Triumph.  
This renders his army temporarily unpaid, but forces the merchant princes to stay and compete for seats to protect their fortunes, while the spectacle strips rival city-states of any casus belli to fund insurgents.

problem: A Shadow Sect spy embedded as an inner disciple in Spirit Affinity House discovers the Immortal Gu 'Fortune Rivalling Heaven' in a secluded pavilion, but it is slated for transfer to the sect's supreme vault in three days. Should they steal it tonight, burning a decade of cover, or wait and hope the transfer provides another chance?
facets: The Immortal Gu is a unique rank 8 treasure that can elevate the spy's cultivation to unmatched heights, but its theft will alert the entire sect and destroy the spy's intelligence network.
After the transfer, the Gu will be guarded by multiple rank 8 Gu Immortals and sealed in a formation that the spy cannot crack without insider authorization, making future theft nearly impossible.

The spy's long-term mission is to provide ongoing information about the sect's secret alliances and to corrupt key disciples; losing this position would jeopardize a decade of Shadow Sect's strategic planning.

angles.directive: Storm the pavilion at night, assassinate the two elder guardians using the Shadow Token Gu, seize the Immortal Gu, and phase through the sect's barrier. Your cover is a small price for this power.

Let the Gu be moved. Observe the transfer route and security, then bide your time. Even if it goes into the vault, you can later use your position to get assigned to vault duty or find a moment of laxity. The risk of permanent loss must be accepted.

Approach the lustful Pavilion Lord with a subtle poison-cum-aphrodisiac, coerce him into removing the Gu for a 'private inspection,' then kill him and pin the theft on a rival elder, keeping your hands clean.

Replace the Immortal Gu with a perfect imitation you spent years crafting, then 'accidentally' trigger a defensive mechanism that incinerates the fake, making the sect believe the Gu was destroyed. You keep the real one hidden, and your cover deepens.

threads: At midnight, they activate the Shadow Token Gu to sever the pavilion’s sound and light, slit the throats of both elder guardians mid-meditation, then phase through the Grand Myriad Barrier with the Gu before the alarm ripple reaches the main hall, converting a decade of intelligence access into immediate rank 8 transcendence.

The spy will volunteer to personally escort the transfer detail, mapping the exact sequence of immortal seals and guard rotations in real time, then request a demotion to vault archivist two months later on grounds of “stagnant cultivation,” which places them inside the formation as a trusted custodian. This burns the current access to the inner disciple rumor mill and corruptible juniors, but converts the sect’s own security into the trapdoor through which Fortune Rivalling Heaven is eventually extracted.

I will dose Pavilion Lord Bai with Heaven-Scented Submission Incense during a private audience, compelling him to retrieve 'Fortune Rivalling Heaven' for a fabricated inspection, then slit his throat and plant the Gu on Elder Sun’s corpse via forged correspondence. The decade-long cover, network, and Gu all remain intact—expending only one elder and a single-use poison.

I swap the Gu for a light-path duplicate I spent years culturing, then trigger the pavilion’s fire-ward to immolate it before witnesses, leaving certified residue. The sect declares the Gu destroyed; my cover gains an ironclad alibi of shocked innocence, while the real Immortal Gu threads into my aperture’s vital gu matrix.

problem: You have seized the hereditary Duchy of Alerio, but the dead duke’s 12-year-old niece—the sole blood-heir—has escaped and is now hidden among peasants who venerate her as a living saint; any move to find her could spark rebellion, yet her survival fuels quiet defiance.
facets: The girl’s innocence and saintly image mean her death would likely create a martyr, galvanizing resistance.
The old dynasty ruled unbroken for three centuries, so the populace sees your rule as illegitimate usurpation.
Your army can crush an open revolt, but a drawn-out insurgency would drain resources and invite rival powers to intervene.

angles.directive: Launch a house-to-house manhunt, rely on informants, and once captured, execute the girl publicly for sedition to extinguish the bloodline’s symbolic power.
Proclaim a general amnesty and pretend she is irrelevant; invest in public festivals and grain subsidies to erode nostalgia for the old house through prosperity and delay.

Privately guarantee her safety, bring her to court under a false identity, and arrange a marriage to your heir, thus absorbing the old bloodline into your dynasty and transforming her into a pledge of continuity.
Obtain a papal decree declaring the fallen duke’s lineage spiritually corrupt and forfeit, recasting your conquest as divine retribution and making hereditary claims sacrilegious.

threads: I would deploy the Crimson Chancery clerks to every parish, compelling householders under pain of attainder to log their kin by name and blood relation against a sealed genealogical register, then cross-reference the entries with the late duke’s court rolls to isolate any unaccounted girl of noble issue. The operation burns through a year’s tithe revenue in informant silver and garrison rations, but it strips the saint-myth of her body before a rival kingdom can weaponize her as a casus belli.

I issue a blanket amnesty that pointedly ignores the niece, then fund a year of weekly grain doles and traveling harvest fairs across every parish.  
The treasury bleeds dry while her myth deflates into irrelevance—peasant loyalty becoming a full stomach, not a saint’s name, until she is just a forgotten girl with no army.

We absorb the girl by smuggling her into the palace as a distant cousin, then betrothing her to my son under a fabricated noble lineage, which retroactively sanctifies our rule as the old dynasty’s hidden continuation. The cost is permanently embedding a potential claimant-queen at the heart of our succession, but the gain is dissolving her saintly insurgency into a legitimizing marriage that makes rebellion against us a betrayal of her own bloodline.

I will dispatch an envoy to Rome to bribe the Curia for a papal bull anathematizing the Alerio line, making the duke’s lineage spiritually corrupt and my conquest divine retribution. The bull transforms the girl’s veneration into heresy, forcing any rebellion to fight both my army and the Church, at the cost of a one-time tribute to the papacy.

problem: Magistrate Shen, newly appointed and hungry to prove his rectitude, has unearthed a ledger of bribes paid years ago by the household in a land-dispute case that left a rival family ruined. He now sends word that unless a 'gift' of five hundred taels is placed in his private account within ten days, these old files will be reopened and the entire household—especially the master—will face public humiliation and possible seizure of property.
facets: The original bribe was arranged through Steward Han, who has since died, leaving the household's direct link to the crime buried but also without a scapegoat to take sole blame.

The household's liquid silver is currently tied up in a shipment of silk that is already three weeks overdue, meaning any large payment would require either selling off stores at a loss or borrowing from a rival merchant at ruinous interest.

The magistrate's own superior, Prefect Guo, is a longtime drinking companion of the master—but involving him could either quash the threat or reveal that the master's protection network has frayed, inviting deeper scrutiny.

angles.directive: Strike first: fabricate or surface evidence that Magistrate Shen himself accepted bribes in a prior post, and deliver it anonymously to the Censorate before his ten-day deadline expires, turning the investigator into the investigated.

Send a trusted junior wife, skilled in flattery and delay, to the magistrate's private residence with a modest 'interim gift' and a promise of the full sum once the silk shipment arrives, while quietly beginning to shift the household's most vulnerable assets out of the prefecture.

Offer Magistrate Shen something more valuable than a one-time payment: a permanent, silent share in the household's pawnbroking business, making his future prosperity dependent on the household's continued good standing and turning a blackmailer into a long-term partner.

Discreetly invite a minor clerk from the magistrate's own yamen to become a 'consultant' to the household at a generous monthly retainer, tasking him to misplace the incriminating ledger pages within the yamen's own archives and shift the blame for any missing records onto bureaucratic disorder rather than the household.

threads: The master inserts a forged receipt of a bribe Shen allegedly solicited into the dead steward’s papers and dispatches an anonymous tip to the Censorate, retroactively casting the household as extortion victims. This gambit burns the steward’s posthumous name and the new evidence’s credibility, but it neutralizes Shen by trapping him in an investigation that renders the original ledger inoperable as a weapon.

The junior wife Madam Liao will deliver a jade-inlaid screen as the interim gift while spinning a tale of the silk caravan’s imminent arrival, buying exactly eighteen days to quietly transfer the deed to the East Granary and the family’s antique scroll collection to a cousin’s name in neighboring Huai Province—the screen’s loss secures the irreplaceable core of the household’s wealth before the magistrate realizes he’s been strung along.

He converts Shen’s extortion into equity by issuing him a bearer certificate for a five-percent silent partnership in the pawnbroking house, with dividends disbursed through a tea merchant intermediary so no coin touches official accounts, thereby trading an immediate liquidity crisis for a permanently compromised prefectural ally whose career now collapses if the household falls.

Plant the bribe ledger pages permanently in the yamen’s dead-record stacks via a retained junior scribe, who then 'discovers' the archival disorder, rendering the document uncertifiable. A modest monthly retainer buys irretrievable bureaucratic nullification of Shen’s leverage, eliminating the need for a ruinous lump-sum payment.

problem: A newly annexed city-state, long accustomed to self-rule, seethes with resentment. Should the prince build a formidable citadel to cow the population, or bet his safety on winning the people’s affection through lower taxes and generous public works?
facets: The city’s populace equates fortresses with foreign tyranny and may erupt into open revolt if construction begins.
A citadel requires vast funds and a garrison whose loyalty is uncertain; neglecting it could reward the people’s loyalty but leave the prince exposed to usurpation by external enemies.
Choosing goodwill demands immediate sacrifices—reduced revenues and concessions—that may embolden rivals inside the court who see such clemency as weakness.

angles.directive: Build a heavily garrisoned citadel in the heart of the city to overawe the populace and eliminate any thought of rebellion.
Publicly refuse to build any fortress, entrusting your safety to the people’s gratitude and deferring all defensive measures to their loyalty.
Marry into the leading local family and appoint their members to key offices, integrating their interests with your rule so you do not need a fortress.
Establish a new, widely admired system of public justice and civic councils that makes the people feel the law is their fortress, rendering physical walls irrelevant.

threads: The prince will requisition the old guildhall at the city’s central crossroads and convert it into a permanent garrison-fortress manned by household troops rotated from the capital every six months to prevent local fraternization. This turns the city’s own circulatory system into a tourniquet, trading a short-term tax levy that strips the merchant class of liquidity for a structural certainty in which every street corner funnels into a kill-zone under his direct command.

By disbanding the royal garrison and redirecting its payroll into a public grain dole administered by neighborhood councils, the prince dissolves the apparatus of occupation. This trades military deterrence for a city that polices itself against external enemies out of material self-interest.

Marry the daughter of the city’s dominant shipwright guildmaster, installing him as harbormaster and his sons as tax assessors and captains of the city guard. The guild’s fortune becomes hostage to your survival; their informants across the docks and markets neutralize conspiracies before they spark, while the cost is a perpetual ten percent share of maritime duties flowing directly to the family’s coffers.

The prince installs elected district tribunals with binding verdicts and citizen councils that control a fifth of the city budget, turning the legal order into a shared possession. This pawns his absolute prerogative for a self-enforcing stability where every citizen becomes a guardian against usurpation, draining the court of its monopoly on violence while making revolt indistinguishable from self-harm.

problem: Frustrated by years of neglect and seeing the household's finances dwindle, Sixth Concubine Golden Lotus secretly sells the master's unreported tax evasion records to a rival merchant family for silver, but the buyer now threatens to expose her unless she provides even more damaging intelligence.
facets: The master's tax evasion records implicate the entire household, including Golden Lotus herself, so exposure would destroy her home and position as well.
